content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 16,
1992
James
C. Jeffrey III
Engineering Consulting Services
P.O.
Box 1492
Santa Cruz,
CA
95061
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—92-610
Dear Mr. Jeffrey:
This is in response to your letter requesting assistance with
respect to your duties as a member of the Santa Cruz Board of
Building Appeals pursuant to the conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Since you are seeking
general guidance,
we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.
2
QUESTION
As a member of the Santa Cruz Board of Building Appeals,
what
restrictions are placed on your ability to represent clients
before city agencies by the Act?
CONCLUSION
You may not make, participate in making,
or otherwise use
your official position to influence a governmental decision in
which you have a financial interest.
However,
you may appear
before agencies,
other than your own agency and agencies appointed
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
q.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-92-610
Page
2
by or subject to the budgetary control of your agency,
so long as
you do not act or purport to act on behalf of,
or as the
representative of,
your agency.
DISCUSSION
Initially, please be aware that the Act does not prohibit
persons from holding public positions.
3
However,
as your city
attorney correctly pointed out, the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act
(Sections 87100 et g., discussed below) g
prohibit a public official from participating in a decision that
will have a financial effect on an economic interest.
However,
this prohibition is applied on a decision—by—decision basis.
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally,
on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on any economic interest
of the official.
Thus,
you may not vote on any matter that will
have a foreseeable and material financial effect on your clients
or your business.
4
(Section 87103(a),
(c),
and (d).)
Moreover,
as your city attorney correctly pointed out, if you
have a conflict of interest with respect to a decision,
you are
not only prohibited from making and participating in the making of
the decision,
but will also be prohibited from using your official
position to influence the decision.
Regulation 18700.1(a)
provides that with regard to a
governmental decision which is within or before the official’s own
agency,
or any agency appointed by or subiect to the budgetary
control of the official’s agency,
the official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence the decision if,
for
the purpose of influencing the decision,
the official contacts,
or
appears before,
or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member,
officer,
employee or consultant of the agency.
5
(Regulations
18700 and 18700.1.)
It is not within the Commission’s jurisdiction to advise on any
other laws that may apply to your situation which may exist
outside of the provisions of the Political Reform Act.
We have enclosed some general materials relating to conflicts
of interest for your information.
Regulation 18700.1(b)
(copy enclosed)
provides exceptions to
this rule.
However,
your city attorney analyzed these exceptions
and concluded they did not apply to your facts.
You have not
provided any information to suggest the contrary.


Our File No.
1—92-610
Page
3
While in some contexts the city is considered a single
agency, with respect to Regulation 18700.1,
the Commission will
generally treat each public body,
even within a single city,
as
separate public bodies.
For example,
in the Levinc4er Advice Letter
(No.
1-88-328,
copy enclosed)
we advised that a member of the Los Gatos Planning
Commission could appear before the city council in his private
capacity.
We stated:
The town council is not under the budgetary or
appointive control of the planning commission.
Therefore,
Mr.
Lien may appear before the town
council regardless of the extent of Mr. Lien’s
economic interest in the subject of the decision
before the town council.
Regulation 18700.1(c) provides, however, that with regard to
a governmental decision of agencies other than the official’s
own
agency,
or agencies appointed by or sublect to the budgetary
control of the official’s agency, the official is attempting to
use his official position to influence the decision if,
for the
purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts or purports
to act on behalf of,
or as the representative of, his or her
agency to any member, officer,
employee or consultant of an
agency.
Such actions include,
but are not limited to the use of
official stationery.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322_5901.6
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Act,ng General Counsel
SH:JWW:aa
Enclosures
6
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of
1Oc per page.
Wallace
Legal Division

or as the
representative of,
your agency.
